content:
Recently I've been working on the drm gem shmem helpers for rust, and while trying to get the patch series ready I ran into a rather painful issue: I wrote two kunit tests for the gem shmem helpers, but building those tests is gated behind a new RUST_DRM_GEM_SHMEM_HELPER option. The problem is that I cannot figure out how on earth to actually get kunit to allow me to enable that option, and I can't be reasonable and use the same config I use for building te kernel generally because kunit gets upset and complains if I try to leave a kernel in the directory I run it in.
To be clear, stuff like (kunit-run is just a script I have that switches to a dedicated kunit directory, checks out the commit in the main directory I'm working in, and then passes whatever options I gave it to the actual script for running kunit):

kunit-run -- --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_PCI_MSI=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_PCI=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_DRM=y --timeout 60 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST_DRM_GEM_SHMEM_HELPER=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_COMPILE_TEST=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_DRM_GEM_SHMEM_HELPER=y

Doesn't appear to work, and just gives me a fun "ERROR:root:Not all Kconfig options selected in kunitconfig were in the generated .config.". I know the reason this is happening is likely a missing config option somewhere, but I also can't be all that productive if I need to do a involved multi-hour kconfig hunt where I hope I manage to figure out what secret dependency is missing.
Is there any more reasonable solution for actually setting kconfig options on kunit runs?

ironically: since writing this I managed to get this config option set, but now CONFIG_RUST doesn't want to be set with no clear reason as to why. so, would definitely be really helpful if there is a method of doing this that involves pulling on my hair less haha
I would suggest double-checking the resolved values of the conditions in menuconfig, i.e. typing / and searching for the particular symbol, then checking the values inside the depends on.
i.e. menuconfig shows what is not being satisfied.
but in order to do that I need to be able to actually get the .config that kunit is generating in the first place
it's not the normal kernel doing this (and even if it was, I've definitely run into many fun situations where there were dependencies that menuconfig was not mentioning)

Lyude said:

but in order to do that I need to be able to actually get the .config that kunit is generating in the first place

Do you mean the .config is getting removed?

Lyude said:

it's not the normal kernel doing this (and even if it was, I've definitely run into many fun situations where there were dependencies that menuconfig was not mentioning)

Not sure what you are referring to, menuconfig should be showing all the values.

By default kunit's python script for running it doesn't allow you to use a kernel tree that has a .config in it. That's why you have to specify extra options over the command line when running the script. It is the .config getting generated from that which I'm having trouble finding, because it seems like it does remove it - and I can't figure out where else the kunit script would be storing it
I know, but it still generates a .config
For instance, by default, it uses .kunit/ as the build folder, and you can also change that
So you should have it in .kunit/.config, unless I am misunderstanding what you are doing.
Regarding menuconfig, I am thinking you may be referring to things it hides in the menus? Going into the search results should still show you those (and why they are not possible to enable).
If they really do not appear in the search, then I think it would be something that did not even get seen by Kconfig, e.g. a config line defined only when building for a given architecture.

By the way, I see the Python script prints the make line (hopefully the right/entire one), so one thing you could do to inspect the .config is copy that make ... O=.kunit ... line and put menuconfig on it instead.
I hope that helps a bit.
Ooo I'll definitely try that when I'm back at my desk again. Thank you!
You're welcome, and please let me know if that doesn't work well!

so yeah - I'm looking through the menuconfig right now for the generated kunit .config, it's still not at all clear to me what's actually preventing some of these options (currently CONFIG_RUST) from being set:

│ Symbol: RUST [=n]
│ Type  : bool
│ Defined at init/Kconfig:2170
│   Prompt: Rust support
│   Depends on: HAVE_RUST [=y] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && (!MODVERSIONS [=n] || GENDWARFKSYMS [=n]) && !GCC_PLUGIN_RANDSTRUCT [=n] && !RANDSTRUCT [=y] && (!DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n] || PAHOLE_HAS_LANG_EXCLUDE [=n] && !LTO [=n]) && (!CFI [=n] || HAVE_CFI_ICALL_NORMALIZE_INTEGERS_RUSTC [=y]) && (!CALL_PADDING [=y] || RUSTC_VERSION [=109401]>=108100) && !KASAN_SW_TAGS [=n] && (!MITIGATION_RETHUNK [=y] || !KASAN [=n] || RUSTC_VERSION [=109401]>=108300)
│   Location:
│ (1) -> General setup
│       -> Rust support (RUST [=n])
│ Selects: EXTENDED_MODVERSIONS [=n] && CFI_ICALL_NORMALIZE_INTEGERS [=n]

OK - I finally noticed the randstruct bit not being set right, set it to Y, got confused again because nothing changed, ended up having to do a diff of the config before/after adding the option to understand what needed to set (it was CONFIG_RANDSTRUCT_NONE=y)
hooray, finally got the test to run :)
thank you miguel!
You're welcome! Very happy to hear it helped :)
